lesson.menu_title: Persuading and Influencing -- Making a Recommendation
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Persuading and Influencing -- Making a Recommendation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Natalie Viegas | Aula 18 -- Persuading and Influencing

listenings.text: Hi Natalie, Tom here, just before you make your case to leadership tomorrow. A quick tip on how to win them over. Do not only list the features. Leadership cares about results, so lead with the recommendation, then back it up. Say something like, what we really need is a faster rollout, not a bigger budget. And remember, a good recommendation is not only clear but also honest about the downside. If you name one small risk yourself, you will sway the room far more than if you pretend there is none. End by asking for buy-in directly. Do not just hope they agree, ask for it. Could you tell me whether I have your support to move ahead? You will do great. Talk tomorrow.

Hi, this is Natalie, recording a quick note after making my recommendation to leadership. I was nervous, but I followed Tom's advice. I did not only describe the plan, I also led with the recommendation and the reason. What we really needed was a faster rollout, so that is where I started. The part that surprised me was the downside. I named one small risk myself, and instead of weakening my case, it actually helped me win them over. They trusted me more because I was honest. I made the case that the new tool was more cost-effective in the long run, and I asked for their buy-in directly at the end. I have learned that influencing is not about pushing harder. It is about being clear, naming the trade-offs honestly, and asking for support. I got the green light.

extra_audio.text: First, you lead with the recommendation, so people hear your main idea right away. Then you give one strong reason and show the benefit. After that, you name one honest downside, because that builds trust and helps you win the room. Next, you handle objections calmly and weigh up the options out loud. Finally, you ask for buy-in directly, instead of just hoping that everyone agrees.
